title: Infinite login redirect loop caused by SameSite=Strict session cookie dropped during SSO callback, compounded by email claim mismatch after Okta-to-Entra migration
problem: After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users experience an infinite redirect loop when logging into TaskFlow. The loop occurs between TaskFlow and Microsoft's login page. The behavior is intermittent — sometimes clearing cookies or using incognito resolves it temporarily, but not reliably. New users with mismatched emails also experience the issue.
root_cause_hypothesis: Two stacked issues: (1) **SameSite=Strict cookie** — TaskFlow sets its session cookie with `SameSite=Strict` during the OAuth callback redirect from Microsoft's domain. Modern browsers silently drop cookies with `SameSite=Strict` on cross-site redirect responses, so the session is never established, and TaskFlow redirects back to the IdP, creating the loop. This is the primary loop mechanism and affects all users non-deterministically. (2) **Email claim mismatch** — Entra ID sends `user.mail` (canonical email like `jane@company.com`) while TaskFlow has users stored with plus-addressed emails (`jane+taskflow@company.com`) or old aliases from Okta. Even when the cookie does stick (browser-dependent timing), TaskFlow cannot match the authenticated identity to a local account for these users, so authentication still fails. The combination explains why ~30% of users are disproportionately affected (they have the email mismatch) and why the behavior is intermittent (cookie dropping depends on browser behavior).
reproduction_steps: 1. Have a user whose TaskFlow account email differs from their Entra ID `user.mail` claim (e.g., plus-addressed email like `jane+taskflow@company.com` in TaskFlow, but `jane@company.com` in Entra)
2. Navigate to TaskFlow login page in a standard browser session
3. Click login — user is redirected to Microsoft Entra ID
5. Microsoft redirects back to TaskFlow's callback URL
6. Observe in browser dev tools: TaskFlow returns a `Set-Cookie` header with `SameSite=Strict; Secure` — browser silently drops the cookie because it arrived via cross-site redirect
7. TaskFlow finds no session cookie on the next request, treats user as unauthenticated, redirects back to Microsoft
environment: TaskFlow served over HTTPS. SSO provider: Microsoft Entra ID (recently migrated from Okta). Entra ID email claim mapped to `user.mail`. Session cookie attributes: `SameSite=Strict; Secure`. Affects approximately 30% of users, particularly those with plus-addressed emails or email aliases that differ between TaskFlow and Entra ID. New hires with pre-provisioned mismatched emails are also affected.
impact: ~30% of the team is completely unable to log into TaskFlow. No reliable workaround exists. New hires with mismatched emails are also locked out. The SSO cookie issue may intermittently affect all users, but those with matching emails may succeed on retry while mismatched-email users face a harder failure.
recommended_fix: **Fix 1 (Cookie — critical, fixes the loop mechanism):** Change the session cookie's `SameSite` attribute from `Strict` to `Lax` (or `None` if Lax is insufficient for the OAuth callback flow, though `Lax` should work for top-level navigations). This is the standard fix for OAuth/SSO callback flows — `SameSite=Strict` cookies are intentionally blocked on cross-site redirects. Look in TaskFlow's session/authentication configuration for where cookie attributes are set. **Fix 2 (Email matching — fixes the 30% user mismatch):** Either (a) update TaskFlow's user lookup to match on a stable, non-email identifier like Entra's `oid` (object ID) claim or `sub` claim instead of email, or (b) remap the Entra ID claim to send a value that matches what TaskFlow stores (e.g., configure a custom claim or use a different attribute), or (c) update the affected users' emails in TaskFlow's database/admin panel to match what Entra sends. Option (a) is the most robust long-term. Option (c) is the fastest short-term fix. Also consider updating the Entra app registration's claims mapping (under Enterprise Applications → TaskFlow → Attributes & Claims) to send a claim that TaskFlow can reliably match on.
proposed_test_case: 1. **Cookie fix verification:** After changing SameSite to Lax, log in with a user whose email matches in both systems — confirm no redirect loop and session cookie persists after the callback redirect. Verify in dev tools that the Set-Cookie header now has `SameSite=Lax`. 2. **Email mismatch fix verification:** After applying the email fix, log in with a user who previously had a plus-addressed email mismatch — confirm they authenticate successfully and are matched to the correct TaskFlow account. 3. **Regression test:** Confirm that users who were previously unaffected can still log in normally after both fixes.
information_gaps: Exact TaskFlow server-side logs during the callback — would confirm whether the app logs a 'user not found' error or a session write error (reporter couldn't access logs)
Which specific TaskFlow framework/library manages sessions and cookie settings — needed to locate the SameSite configuration
Whether any users with matching emails also experience the loop (would isolate the cookie issue from the email issue)
TaskFlow version and authentication library in use
